perturbation.original_text: Recipient’s duty of non -disclosure under this Agreement shall extend beyond the term of thi s Agreement for a period of three (3) years from the date of last disclosure.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient’s duty of non-disclosure under this Agreement shall extend beyond the term of this Agreement for a period of one (1) year from the date of last disclosure.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the non-disclosure period to one year may not adequately protect trade secrets, particularly in California, where trade secret misappropriation laws offer broader protection.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) provides protection for trade secrets for as long as they remain trade secrets. Reducing the contractual protection to one year may be insufficient if the information retains its trade secret status for a longer duration, potentially conflicting with the protections offered by CUTSA. The modified text creates a legal contradiction because the state law offers longer protection if the data constitutes a trade secret. CUTSA provides a remedy for misappropriation as long as the information qualifies as a trade secret, which can extend beyond one year.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the non-disclosure period from three years to one year. The Law Explanation states that California's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) protects trade secrets as long as they remain trade secrets, potentially longer than one year. Scraped Law Snippet 1, citing California Civil Code 3426, confirms the existence of the Uniform Trade Secrets Act in California. Therefore, reducing the contractual protection to one year could contradict CUTSA if the information remains a trade secret beyond that period.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement may be terminated by either party upon thirty (30) days written notice to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement may be terminated by either party upon ten (10) days written notice to the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 10 days could be problematic if it involves potential impacts to employees who have vested benefits.  California law requires a longer notice period in some situations.
perturbation.law_explanation: The California Worker Adjustment and Retraining Notification (WARN) Act requires 60 days' advance notice of a plant closing or mass layoff. If the NDA's termination impacts employees triggering the WARN Act, the 10-day notice period would be a violation. The modified text creates a legal contradiction because terminating the NDA with only 10 days' notice could potentially trigger mass layoffs, thereby violating the CA WARN Act’s requirement for a 60-day notice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Codes Display Text skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Group Code Section Group Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ Add To My Favorites Code Text LAB Labor Code - LAB
perturbation.justification: The changed text reduces the termination notice period from 30 to 10 days, which contradicts the California WARN Act's requirement for a 60-day notice as explained in the Law Explanation. The Perturbation Explanation highlights potential impacts to employees who have vested benefits, which further supports the contradiction. Scraped Law Snippet 1 identifies the Labor Code (LAB) as relevant, implying the existence of employment-related legal obligations. This aligns with the scenario where mass layoffs occur due to the termination of the agreement, and the 10-day notice is not enough time to comply with WARN requirements. Since the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction arises when 'a contract sets a deadline or requirement that violates federal or state law, making the contractual terms unenforceable or illegal,' the modification qualifies as a legal contradiction because it violates the WARN Act under certain circumstances.

perturbation.original_text: Within ten (10) days after any written request by Sanmina, the Recipient shall promptly return all copies of the Confidential Information except copies required for backup, disaster recovery, or business continuity and in such case the obligations hereunder shall survive until such copies are destroyed .
perturbation.changed_text: Within thirty (30) days after any written request by Sanmina, the Recipient shall return all copies of the Confidential Information except copies required for backup, disaster recovery, or business continuity; these copies may be retained indefinitely.
perturbation.explanation: Allowing indefinite retention of confidential information for backup, disaster recovery, or business continuity purposes contradicts the principle of limiting the duration of confidentiality obligations, which increases the risk of misuse or unauthorized disclosure.
perturbation.law_explanation: While California law protects trade secrets, it also implies a reasonableness standard regarding the duration of that protection, especially when balanced against other considerations. Allowing indefinite retention of confidential information, even for backup purposes, increases the risk of eventual disclosure or misuse, potentially undermining the trade secret status and the reasonableness of the protective measures. The modified text contradicts trade secret law, because trade secrets are protected as long as reasonable efforts are made to maintain secrecy. Keeping backups indefinitely might be seen as not reasonable.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426. This title may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1.)
perturbation.justification: The changed text allows indefinite retention of confidential information for backup, disaster recovery, or business continuity purposes, which contradicts the principle that trade secrets are protected as long as reasonable efforts are made to maintain secrecy. The Law Explanation states indefinite retention might be seen as not reasonable. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that the title is the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. The Perturbation Explanation says that it increases the risk of misuse or unauthorized disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: Wherever one party is required or permitted or required to give written notice to the other under this Agreement, such notice will be given by hand, by certified U.S. mail, return rec eipt requested, by overnight courier, or by fax and addressed to the appropriate officer of such party (with a copy to the Legal Department) at the address set forth in the preamble. All such notices shall be effective upon receipt. Either party may desi gnate a different notice address from time to time upon giving five (5) days’ prior written notice thereof to the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: Wherever one party is required to give written notice to the other under this Agreement, such notice will be given exclusively via Twitter direct message to the other party's designated account. All such notices shall be effective immediately upon sending the direct message. Either party may designate a different Twitter account for notice by updating their profile.
perturbation.explanation: Mandating notice exclusively via Twitter direct message is not a legally sound or reliable method for providing notice, particularly for critical legal documents. This is because Twitter is not a secure channel and there is no guarantee of receipt or confirmation that the intended party actually received or read the message. Additionally, Twitter's terms of service may change, affecting the reliability of this method.
perturbation.law_explanation: California's UETA requires that electronic records and signatures be reliable. A Twitter DM doesn't fulfill that requirement. The modified text introduces a legally unsound procedure that likely fails to meet the standards for reliable notification under California's UETA, especially concerning sensitive legal or confidential information exchanged between businesses. There's no reliable confirmation of receipt or guarantee of security, making it an unsuitable method for legal notification. The modification is also contradictory as it may not provide 'conspicuous' notice as required by UETA.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 1633.1 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 2. CONTRACTS [1549 - 1701] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 2.5. ELECTRONIC TRANSACTIONS [1633.1 - 1633.17] ( Title 2.5 added by Stats. 1999, Ch. 428, Sec. 1. ) 1633.1. This title may be cited as the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act. (Added by Stats. 1999, Ch. 428, Sec. 1. Effective January 1, 2000.)
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text, by mandating notice exclusively via Twitter direct message, contradicts the principles of reliable electronic communication as suggested by California's UETA, referenced via California Civil Code 1633.1, which is the Uniform Electronic Transactions Act. The law explanation states that Twitter DM doesn't fulfill the UETA requirement that electronic records and signatures be reliable because there's no reliable confirmation of receipt or guarantee of security, rendering it unsuitable for legal notification, which directly opposes the standard of electronic communication required by UETA.
